summarization.a: Conceptual diagram defining the distance metrics for analyzing SMI bonding configurations. d_init is the initial inter-silanol distance on the free surface, d_bond is the distance between surface oxygen atoms after forming a doubly bonded (DB) SMI, and Δd is their absolute difference representing the structural distortion required for bonding.
summarization.b: Scatter plot showing electronic energy (ΔE_elec) versus Δd for DB SMI configurations, referenced to the most stable A.1-O:13 configuration. Below Δd = 1.2 Å, energies remain moderate (~50 kJ/mol). Beyond this threshold, ΔE_elec increases dramatically (>100 kJ/mol), indicating large energy penalties for DB formation when significant silanol rearrangement is required.
summarization.c: Scatter plot showing ΔE_elec versus d_bond for SB/SB configurations (two singly bonded SMIs sharing adjacent silanols), referenced to geminal A.1-O:34 (empty circle). Electronic stability increases (more negative ΔE_elec) with larger d_bond, indicating that greater separation between two SB SMIs reduces steric repulsion.
summarization.d: Scatter plot showing ΔE_elec versus Δd for SB/SB configurations. Larger Δd correlates with less negative ΔE_elec, showing that surface distortion to accommodate two adjacent SMIs imposes an energy penalty that partially offsets the stabilization from increased separation.

d.question: What is represented by the empty circle in panels (c) and (d)?
d.answer: The empty circle represents the geminal A.1-O:34 SB/SB configuration, which serves as the reference point (ΔE_elec = 0) for comparing other SB/SB configurations.

c.question: How do these energy relationships guide the prediction of optimal SMI blocking layer configurations?
c.answer: The data show that short inter-silanol distances (d_init < 4 Å) favor DB configuration formation, while larger distances favor two separate SB SMIs. By analyzing the silanol distribution on a given surface, one can predict which binding motif will be energetically preferred at each site. This allows systematic derivation of the most stable full-coverage blocking layer without computing all possible permutations, by simply matching silanol pair distances to the appropriate bonding mode.

b.question: What does the Δd = 1.2 Å threshold in panel (b) indicate about DB SMI formation?
b.answer: This threshold marks the boundary between feasible and unfeasible doubly bonded (DB) SMI configurations. When Δd < 1.2 Å, the surface silanols are already close enough that minimal structural rearrangement is needed for one TMPS molecule to bond to both, resulting in moderate energy penalties. When Δd > 1.2 Å (corresponding to initial inter-silanol distances ≥4 Å), the silica framework must deform substantially to bring the oxygens into bonding distance, causing ΔE_elec to exceed 100 kJ/mol and making DB formation kinetically and thermodynamically unfavorable.

b.question: Why do SB/SB configurations become more stable at larger d_bond values?
b.answer: Greater separation between two singly bonded SMIs reduces steric repulsion between their substituents (propyl and methoxy groups), lowering the electronic energy and stabilizing the configuration.
